125From: Methods in Molecular Biology, vol 372: Mitochondria: Practical Protocols
Edited by: D Leister and J M Herrmann © Humana Press Inc., Totowa, NJ9
Isolation of Intact, Functional Mitochondria 
From the Model Plant Arabidopsis thaliana
Lee J Sweetlove, Nicolas L Taylor, and Christopher J Leaver
Summary
The ability to isolate intact, functional mitochondria from plant tissues is a key tech-
nique in the study of the genome, proteome, and metabolic function of the plant mitochon-
drion Traditionally, mitochondrial plant researchers have turned to specific plant systemsand organs (such as potato tubers and pea shoots) from which mitochondria are readily iso-lated in large quantities However, increasingly, research is focused on a small number ofmodel species, and there is a need to adapt existing protocols to allow the isolation of mito-chondria from these model species Arguably, the most important of these is Arabidopsis
thaliana , for which a formidable array of genetic resources is available However, because
of its relatively small size and the absence of large heterotrophic organs, Arabidopsis is a
challenging plant from which to isolate mitochondria Here, we present two methods forisolating mitochondria from Arabidopsis , either from heterotrophic cell suspension 
cultures or from hydroponic seedling cultures We also present details of commonly usedassays to assess the physical and functional integrity of the isolated organelles Key Words: Arabidopsis ; cell suspension culture; leaf; mitochondria isolation; outer
mitochondrial membrane integrity; respiratory control ratio 1 Introduction
Arabidopsis thaliana was the first plant in which all three cellular genomes
(nuclear, mitochondrial, plastidic) were sequenced (1), and the subsequent
development of a range of genetic resources has established its position as the
model plant species Both reverse and forward genetic mutagenesis strategies
are now routine, and the former can be achieved at high throughput and withgenomic saturation (2) Although these genetic approaches are facilitating the
discovery of gene function in Arabidopsis , increasingly the precise dissection
of gene function requires a combination of genetic and biochemical approaches.126 Sweetlove, Taylor, and Leaver
Among these, the ability to isolate and study the genome, proteome, and bio-
chemistry of key organelles such as the mitochondrion is paramount (3) This is
particularly true of plants in which the degree of subcellular compartmentationis high and leads to a unique metabolic and biochemical complexity The choice of Arabidopsis as a model species is largely driven by genetic fac-
tors Although the applicability of Arabidopsis as a genetic model is unchal-
lenged, it is a far from ideal plant for conducting biochemical investigations Themain problem is that Arabidopsis is small and therefore yields much less experi-
mental material than larger crop plants such as maize, soybean, and potato, whichhave traditionally been the organisms of choice for biochemists Thus, it has beennecessary to adapt existing biochemical assays to overcome the constraint of lim-ited starting material For example, highly sensitive amplifying assays have beendeveloped to allow the measurement of a large number of enzymes fromArabidopsis leaf material (4) In the context of isolating mitochondria from
Arabidopsis , as well as the constraint of limited starting material, there is also the
problem that Arabidopsis has little nonphotosynthetic tissue (root and seed mass
are a relatively small proportion of the total plant mass) Isolation of mitochon-dria from photosynthetic plant tissues is inherently more difficult than from non-photosynthetic tissue because of contamination from the thylakoid membranesystem of the chloroplast, which has a similar density to mitochondria In this chapter, we present two methods for isolating mitochondria from
Arabidopsis using density gradient centrifugation The first uses a hetero-
trophic cell suspension culture to minimize the problem of thylakoid conta-mination (5), and the second uses Arabidopsis seedlings grown in large
quantities using a simple shaking hydroponic culture system and a specificgradient of Percoll and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) designed to separate mito-chondria from thylakoids (6) 2 Materials
2.1 Growth of Heterotrophic Arabidopsis Cell Suspension Cultures
Culture medium (1 L): MS salts including vitamins (Ducheefa, Haarlem, The
Netherlands), 3% (w/v) sucrose, 0.5 mg napthalene acetic acid (from stock of 1 mg/mL in water), 50 Rg kinetin (from stock of 1 mg/mL in water) Adjust 
pH to 5.8 with KOH and autoclave 2.2 Growth of Hydroponic Arabidopsis Seedling Cultures
1 Glass culture vessels (100 mL) with a polypropylene Magenta B-cap (Sigma-
Aldrich Company Ltd., Poole, Dorset, UK) Sterilize by autoclaving 2 Culture medium (500 mL): MS salts including vitamins (Ducheefa), 2% (w/v)
sucrose, 0.2 g MES Adjust pH to 5.8 with KOH Transfer 60-mL aliquots to 
sterile glass culture vessels; add 0.06 g agar and autoclave.3 Sterilizing solution 1: 70% (v/v) ethanol 4 Sterilizing solution 2: 5% (v/v) sodium hypochlorite, 0.1% (v/v) Tween-20.5 Sterile distilled water 2.3 Preparation of Density Gradients
1 Gradient buffer 1: 0.6 Mmannitol, 20 m MTES-KOH, pH 7.5, 0.2% (w/v) bovine
serum albumin (BSA) 2
summary:
Revised Summary:
"Mitochondria: Practical Protocols" by D. Leister and J. M. Herrmann in Methods in Molecular Biology, vol. 372, provides detailed methods for isolating intact, functional mitochondria from model plants. The book focuses on the challenges of working with small-sized model plant species, such as Arabidopsis thaliana, which have limited starting material and complex subcellular compartmentation. The protocols cover the growth of heterotrophic cell suspension cultures and hydroponic seedling cultures, as well as the isolation of mitochondria using density gradient centrifugation. The book also discusses the importance of maintaining the physical and functional integrity of the isolated organelles and provides techniques to assess these parameters. This resource is valuable for mitochondrial plant researchers working with model plant species.

Isolation of intact, functional mitochondria from plant tissues using density
gradient centrifugation has been a standard procedure for many years For a
general practical guide, consult ref.7 The key aspects to the methodology are
disruption of plant cells without rupture of the organelles, protection oforganelles from harmful compounds released from other subcellular compart-ments, and separation of mitochondria from other organelles by density gradi-ent centrifugation We describe methods to isolate mitochondria fromArabidopsis tissue In addition, methods are described to assess organelle
integrity and the extent of maintenance of normal respiratory function 3.1 Growth of Heterotrophic Arabidopsis Cell Suspension Cultures
1 Cell suspension cultures are established from Arabidopsis callus material (8) Mature cultures are maintained under sterile conditions with 100 mL culture
medium in 250-mL conical flasks sealed with aluminum foil ( seeNote 1 ) Flasks
are shaken on an orbital shaker at 120 rpm and maintained at 22°C under illumi-nation (16-h photoperiod, 110 Rmol photons/m
2/s) 2 Cells are subcultured after 7 d of growth Working in a laminar flow hood, a sterile
plastic pipet is used to withdraw 10 mL culture (swirl the flask directly beforedoing this to prevent cells from settling) This aliquot of the culture is then pipetedinto 100 mL fresh culture medium in a clean, sterile, 250-mL conical flask 3 Stock cultures (generally five or six 250-mL flasks) are maintained under illumi-
nated conditions To prepare fully heterotrophic cultures, subculture from thestock cultures as above and grow in dark conditions As many as eight or nine
culture flasks can be generated from a single stock culture For mitochondrial iso-lation, 10 cultures (250 mL each) are an ideal starting point Mitochondria are bestisolated at d 6 when the cells are still in the logarithmic phase of growth 3.2 Growth of Hydroponic Arabidopsis Seedling Cultures
1 This method is adapted from ref.9 2 Place 7 mg seeds in a 1.5-mL microtube All subsequent steps should be per-
formed using sterile technique in a laminar flow hood Add 1 mL sterilizationsolution 1 Incubate for 2 min and remove solution by pipeting Add 1 mL sterili-zation solution 2 and incubate for 15 min, mixing by inversion every 5 min.Remove solution by pipeting and wash seeds with 1 mL sterile distilled waterthree times Resuspend seeds in 0.5 mL water Using a blue pipet with the distal 5 mm cut off, gently pipet the seeds onto the surface of the culture medium inglass culture vessels ( seeNote 2 ) 3
summary:
Revised Summary (Iteration 1):

This article provides a detailed guide on isolating mitochondria from Arabidopsis tissue using density gradient centrifugation. It covers the isolation methods for heterotrophic Arabidopsis cell suspension cultures and hydroponic Arabidopsis seedling cultures. The article also includes techniques for assessing mitochondrial integrity and respiratory function. Key entities involved in the process include gradient buffer 2, Miracloth, muslin, Buchner funnel, vacuum pump, cell extraction medium, Beckman JA-14 or equivalent, cell mitochondria wash medium, a paintbrush, a 5-mL pipet, a 40/23/18% Percoll step gradient, sterile distilled water, a Perspex vessel, seedling extraction medium, a Polytron® homogenizer, leaf mitochondria wash medium, a 28% Percoll solution, a Clark-type oxygen electrode, stock solutions of respiratory substrates, and density gradient centrifugation. Several companies and brands, such as Amersham Biosciences UK Ltd., Merck Biosciences, Beckman JA-14, Sweetlove, Hansatech, and Rank Brothers, are associated with the techniques and equipment used in the experiments. The growth of Heterotrophic Arabidopsis Cell Suspension Cultures involves the use of Arabidopsis callus material, 250-mL conical flasks, aluminum foil, an orbital shaker, and a photoperiod of Rmol photons/m2/s. The growth of Hydroponic Arabidopsis Seedling Cultures involves the use of 1.5-mL microtubes, sterilization solutions, sterile distilled water, a blue pipet, culture medium, and glass culture vessels. Density gradient centrifugation is used for the isolation of intact mitochondria. The growth of both cell suspension cultures and seedling cultures is carried out in dark conditions during the logarithmic phase of growth.

summary:
Culture vessels are shaken on an orbital shaker at 80 rpm and maintained at 22°C under illumination (16-h photoperiod, 110 Rmol photons/m2/s). Seedlings should be harvested between 10 and 14 days later.

For the preparation of density gradients:
- Appropriate concentrations of Percoll are prepared in the gradient buffer.
- Gradients should be prepared in advance and stored on ice until required.
- Two different gradients are used for isolating mitochondria from heterotrophic cell suspension cultures.
- The first gradient consists of 5 mL 40% Percoll overlaid with 20 mL 23% Percoll and then 10 mL 18% Percoll, all made up using gradient buffer 1.
- The second gradient consists of 30 mL 28% Percoll made up in gradient buffer 2.
- For isolating mitochondria from Arabidopsis hydroponic seedling cultures, a single linear PVP-40 gradient in 28% Percoll is used.
- The preparation of this gradient requires a gradient maker with two chambers connected by a small pipe.
- Two solutions are prepared: a "heavy" solution of 28% Percoll and 4.4% PVP-40, and a "light" solution of 28% Percoll.
- The gradient is poured by opening the taps on both the connecting pipe and the outflow simultaneously.
- The level of solution in both chambers should drop at the same rate.

For the isolation of mitochondria from heterotrophic Arabidopsis cell suspension cultures:
- All plasticware and glassware must be free from traces of detergent.
- All procedures should be done at 4°C, with prechilled solutions, and in a cold room.
- Cells are separated from the culture medium by filtration through Miracloth.
- The cells are then transferred to a blender vessel and blended with cell extraction medium.
- The extract is filtered to remove starch, cell debris, and unbroken cells.
- The filtrate is centrifuged, and the supernatant is poured off and discarded.
- The pellet is washed with cell mitochondria wash medium and centrifuged again.
- The process is repeated once more.

it is essential that all plasticware and glassware be free from traces of detergent
All procedures should be done at 4°C
and the work should be done in a cold room
Centrifugation should be done using a refrigerated centrifuge
Separate cells from culture medium by filtration through a single layer of Miracloth using a Buchner funnel and a vacuum pump
Transfer up to 150 g FW of cells to a 500-mL Waring blender vessel
Add at least 2 volumes of cell extraction medium per weight of cells
and blend for 15 s at high speed and twice for 15 s at low speed
Leave 30-s intervals between bursts of blending to prevent excessive foaming and heating
Filter extract by wringing through one layer of Miracloth and two layers of muslin to remove starch
Transfer filtrate to 250-mL centrifuge tubes and centrifuge at 1500 g for 10 min
Gently pour resulting supernatant into fresh centrifuge tubes
Take care not to transfer any of the pellet
Centrifuge the supernatant from step 5 at 18
Pour off and discard the resultant supernatant
Add 1 mL cell mitochondria wash medium to the pellet and gently resuspend using a small paintbrush
Transfer all resuspended pellets to a single 50-mL centrifuge tube and add wash medium to 30 mL

summary:
Summary: Prepare the organelle suspension by washing cell mitochondria with 20 mL of wash medium. Centrifuge at 18 and remove the supernatant. Resuspend the mitochondrial pellet in 1 mL of wash medium. 

To extract mitochondria from Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings, homogenize the tissue using a Polytron homogenizer. Filter the extract and centrifuge it at 1100 g for 5 min. Pour the supernatant into fresh tubes, avoiding the pellet. Centrifuge the supernatant at 18 and resuspend the pellets in 1 mL of leaf mitochondria wash medium.

For density gradient centrifugation, carefully pipet the organelle suspension onto the 40/23/18% step gradient. Centrifuge at 40. Mitochondria will appear as a white/pale brown band at the interface of the 40/23% Percoll layers. Aspirate the upper part of the gradient and add 20 mL of wash medium to the mitochondria. Centrifuge at 18 and remove the supernatant. Repeat the process with a 28% Percoll gradient.

To preserve the integrity of the isolated mitochondria, all procedures should be done at 4°C.

summary:
Revised Summary:
During the process of mitochondrial isolation, it is necessary to remove the supernatant, leaving 3-4 mL in the tube. Sweetlove, Taylor, and Leaver recommend repeating this step and pouring off the resultant supernatant. Additionally, 0.5 mL of leaf mitochondria wash medium should be added, and the pellet should be gently resuspended with a small paintbrush. Mitochondrial integrity can be assessed by measuring the latency of the assay of COX to added cytochrome-c, which is an inner membrane respiratory complex. The outer mitochondrial membrane needs to be ruptured for COX activity. The appearance of organelle bands on different density gradients can indicate the integrity of the outer mitochondrial membrane. COX activity, measured as the cytochrome-c-dependent consumption of oxygen, can be used to estimate outer mitochondrial membrane integrity. The oxygen electrode used for measuring COX activity should be set up with saturated KCl as an electrolyte. The respiratory control ratio can be calculated using the dependence on added ADP. Oxygen consumption can be measured using the oxygen electrode. To prevent contamination and ensure cell growth, it is important to clean and sterilize flasks and glass culture vessels, keep seeds close to the surface of the medium, and use dedicated detergent-free glassware and plasticware. Sufficient extraction medium is necessary to prevent organelle rupture during mitochondrial extraction. When wringing extracts through muslin/Miracloth, gentle pressure should be applied. It is also important to correctly identify the mitochondrial band on the density gradient.

summary: Summary (Iteration 3): Oxygen electrodes deteriorate over time due to the deposition of chloride and oxide salts on the silver anode, necessitating periodic cleaning with aluminum oxide polishing paste. To prevent excessive oxygen consumption, avoid adding too much mitochondrial suspension, and test different concentrations to optimize consumption rate. Freshly prepare the Na-ascorbate solution for accurate measurements. Financial support for this research was provided by the Biotechnological and Biological Sciences Research Council and a long-term EMBO fellowship. Utilize pictures of organelles separated by density gradient centrifugation from Arabidopsis cell suspension cultures and hydroponic seedling culture tissue. Analyze the genome sequence of Arabidopsis thaliana and develop a high-throughput reverse genetics system. Conduct proteomics analysis to survey intracellular real estate in plants, and study enzyme activities and transcript levels during diurnal cycles and prolonged darkness. Investigate the Arabidopsis mitochondrial proteome and characterize chlorophyll-free mitochondria from pea leaves. Isolate and subfractionate mitochondria from plants. Study the oxidative stimulation of glutathione synthesis in Arabidopsis suspension cultures and the coordinated response of glutathione metabolic genes to heavy metals and jasmonic acid.
